issueSetup.Intro: Most weeks, the problem is not too little information — it is too much. This issue looks at how a few deliberate workflows can turn the daily flood of articles, tools, and updates into something you can actually use with students and teams. Less noise, more signal.
issueSetup.In This Issue: Three short reads on building sustainable AI habits, plus a tool worth trying and one trend to watch.
issueSetup.Closing Reflection: Pick one workflow from this issue and run it for a week. The goal is not to do more — it is to make the work you already do a little lighter and a lot more intentional. What is the smallest loop you could close this week?

articles.Short Navigation Title: Start With the Loop, Not the Tool
articles.Title: Start With the Loop, Not the Tool
articles.Key Facts / Summary: Teams often adopt a new AI tool before they have named the workflow it is meant to serve. Mapping the loop first — capture, summarize, decide, share — makes it obvious which tool (if any) is actually needed and where time is really lost.
articles.Why It Matters: When the workflow is clear, tool choices get simpler and training gets shorter. Educators spend less time chasing features and more time on the parts only a human can do: judgment, context, and care.
articles.Practical Takeaways: Draw your current content workflow on one page before adopting anything new.|Name the single step where time leaks most, and target only that.|Pilot one workflow with one team for two weeks before scaling.
articles.Author: Jordan Avery
articles.Image Description: A simple hand-drawn loop diagram on a whiteboard with four labeled stages

articles.Title: Writing AI Summaries People Actually Trust
articles.Key Facts / Summary: An AI-generated summary is only useful if readers believe it. The most trusted digests pair a short original summary with a clear link to the source and a note on what was left out, so readers can verify in seconds.
articles.Why It Matters: Trust is the currency of any publication. A transparent summary respects readers' time and protects the original author, while still giving busy educators a fast way in.
articles.Practical Takeaways: Always link to the original source, not a search result.|Keep summaries short enough that readers still want the full piece.|Add a one-line note on uncertainty or scope when it matters.
articles.Author: Priya Nandakumar
articles.Image Description: A clean side-by-side of a short summary card next to a linked source article

articles.Title: From a Spreadsheet to a Polished Publication
articles.Key Facts / Summary: Keeping issue content in a structured spreadsheet — one row per item, with clear columns — lets a small script turn raw notes into a formatted newsletter automatically. The spreadsheet stays the single source of truth.
articles.Why It Matters: Separating content from layout means anyone on the team can contribute without touching code, and the published result stays consistent issue after issue.
articles.Practical Takeaways: Use one column per field: summary, why it matters, takeaways, source.|Mark each row Include? Yes/No so drafts and finals live together.|Let the layout be generated, so editors focus on the writing.
articles.Author: Marcus Lee
articles.Image Description: A spreadsheet on the left with an arrow pointing to a formatted newsletter on the right

optionalSections.Content: Take last week's saved links and write a two-sentence original summary for each. Notice which ones you can no longer remember why you saved — those are candidates to drop.
Watch for tools that promise to replace editorial judgment entirely. The durable wins are the ones that speed up capture and formatting while keeping a human firmly in the decision loop.
